content:
7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Ask an AI coding agent to implement a standard CRUD endpoint and it may return a reviewable pull request with limited human involvement.
Ask the same agent to diagnose an intermittent production failure in an unfamiliar system and it may spend 30 minutes developing a convincing solution to the wrong problem.
The difference is not simply model capability. It is the shape of the task.
A CRUD endpoint usually has a familiar pattern, defined inputs, observable outputs, and established tests. A production failure may depend on incomplete logs, undocumented infrastructure behaviour, changing hypotheses, or a business rule known only to someone who has worked on the platform for years.
Both tasks can benefit from AI, but they require different operating modes.
The endpoint may be delegated as a bounded assignment. The production incident requires a tight loop in which a developer reviews each finding, tests each assumption, and redirects the investigation as new evidence appears.
An AI agent earns autonomy when the task has been scoped tightly enough to make independent execution useful, reviewable, and safe.
Speed and autonomy are separate decisions
Teams often treat speed and autonomy as one measure. A more capable model is assumed to deserve more freedom.
In practice, these are separate dimensions.
Interactivity describes how frequently a person evaluates and redirects the model. A developer gives an instruction, receives an answer, tests it, and determines the next step.
Autonomy describes how far the model proceeds before a human reviews its work. An agent may inspect files, plan a change, modify the codebase, run tests, correct failures, and return with a completed result.
Production troubleshooting can require fast interaction but permit little autonomy because each finding may change the direction of the investigation. A clearly specified API integration may take longer but support substantial autonomy because the expected result and review criteria are already known.
Research into approximately 400,000 Claude Code sessions found a similar division of responsibility. People made most of the planning decisions about what should be done, while the agent made more of the execution decisions about how to do it.
The two modes also require different engineering strengths.
Interactive work rewards developers who can recognize weak assumptions, interpret incomplete evidence, and change direction quickly.
Autonomous delegation rewards developers who can define the objective, supply the necessary context, set boundaries, describe failure cases, and establish acceptance criteria before execution begins.
Teams need both capabilities.
Autonomous execution works on a defined class of tasks
Coding agents can now navigate repositories, modify several files, run commands, inspect test failures, and revise their own work.
Research organizations such as METR assess this capability through software tasks of increasing duration and difficulty. The important detail is that these tasks are typically self-contained, well specified, and paired with clear success criteria.
The same principle appears in SWE-bench, where tasks are reviewed to ensure that the problem description is clear, the tests are appropriate, and the issue can be solved using the available information.
These evaluations demonstrate genuine progress. They also show the conditions under which autonomy works best:

• The objective is explicit.

• Relevant context is available.

• The expected output is known.

• Success can be verified.

• The work does not depend heavily on hidden organizational knowledge.
Real engineering work rarely arrives in that condition. Someone has to create it.
The team earns the time savings by doing the scoping work first.
More freedom does not repair an unclear task
Imagine that an agent receives this instruction:
Modernize the authentication flow and improve token security.
The request sounds reasonable, but it leaves several decisions unresolved.
The agent might replace the token mechanism, introduce a new dependency, change the database schema, and generate a comprehensive test suite. The pull request may compile and pass every test it created.
During review, the team discovers that the implementation removed a legacy session rule required by several enterprise customers. The rule was never documented and never included in the brief.
The agent completed the task it inferred. The team needed a different task completed.
A stronger instruction would be:
Add refresh-token rotation to the existing authentication service. Use the current token repository and encryption library. Preserve SSO and enterprise session behaviour. Do not change the database schema or public authentication interface. Revoke the previous refresh token after successful rotation. Add tests for valid rotation, expired tokens, reused tokens, and SSO sessions.
The second version gives the agent less freedom, but a greater chance of producing a useful result. It identifies the required change, the components to reuse, the behaviour that must remain intact, the boundaries of the task, and the evidence a reviewer expects.
Autonomy improves when ambiguity decreases.
The scoping test before delegation
Before starting an autonomous run, a developer should be able to answer five questions.
What outcome should the agent produce?
The expected result must be concrete. “Improve the service” allows too many interpretations. “Add idempotency handling to the payment endpoint using the existing request-key table” gives the agent a defined target.
What context does the agent need?
Relevant context may include architecture decisions, API contracts, business rules, security requirements, data models, previous incidents, and operational constraints.
The agent cannot reliably account for information it never received.
Which boundaries must it respect?
The brief should identify which files, interfaces, schemas, dependencies, and behaviours may change. It should also state what must remain untouched.
How will success be verified?
Acceptance criteria should be observable. They may include passing tests, required API responses, performance limits, security checks, logging expectations, compatibility requirements, or specific review points.
“Looks correct” is not an acceptance criterion.
When should the agent stop and ask?
The agent should pause when it encounters an architectural conflict, an unclear business rule, a destructive migration, a security-sensitive decision, or a condition outside the supplied scope.
An agent that knows when to stop can often be trusted with more autonomy than one expected to resolve every uncertainty independently.
Work can move between modes
A task does not need to remain interactive or autonomous throughout its lifecycle.
A feature may begin as an open question. The developer and model explore approaches, inspect the architecture, identify dependencies, and clarify the expected behaviour.
Once uncertainty has been reduced, the work can be divided into bounded tasks and delegated.
A productive sequence often looks like this:
Explore interactively → make the decisions → define the boundaries → delegate execution → review the result
Consider a third-party integration. During the interactive phase, the developer may use the model to understand the provider’s authentication method, compare integration patterns, identify rate limits, decide how errors should map into the platform, and define the internal client interface.
Once those decisions are settled, the agent may independently implement the client, add retry logic, write tests, and update the documentation.
Human judgment remains central. It is applied before execution to define the work and after execution to verify the result.
Over-delegation creates convincing rework
The most immediate risk is not that agents will replace developers. It is that teams will delegate tasks before those tasks are ready.
A vague assignment produces a large pull request. The code is structured, the documentation is polished, and the tests pass. The output appears complete.
It may still contain decisions the team never made.
The agent may introduce a dependency that conflicts with platform standards, create a new abstraction where an approved one already exists, overlook operational failure paths, or write tests that confirm its own interpretation of the requirement.
Generation time appears to have been saved. Review time then increases because a senior engineer must reconstruct the agent’s assumptions and trace their consequences through the codebase.
Polished output makes this harder. Weak code invites scrutiny. Fluent, internally consistent code can move through review before anyone challenges the premise behind it.
Reviewing autonomous work therefore requires more than checking syntax and tests. The reviewer must ask whether the agent solved the intended problem, remained inside the approved scope, preserved existing architecture, considered failure paths, and avoided new security or maintenance risks.
The cost of choosing the wrong mode is uneven. Keeping a well-defined task interactive may waste several minutes. Delegating an ambiguous task can introduce a plausible defect that survives review and reaches production.
Autonomy should be earned task by task
There is no single autonomy setting for an engineering team, a repository, or even one agent.
The same coding agent may independently implement a routine endpoint, collaborate closely on a difficult refactor, and remain advisory during a production incident.
The correct level depends on task clarity, available context, technical risk, hidden business knowledge, reversibility, observability, and verification cost.
This operating decision can matter more than the model selected.
A highly capable agent working from an incomplete brief can create extensive rework. A more limited agent working inside a precise scope may produce a result the team can verify and accept quickly.
Teams that gain durable value from coding agents do not measure progress by how much work they delegate. They examine how often agent output is aligned, reviewable, maintainable, and connected to the intended result.
Sustainable AI-assisted engineering depends on more than model capability. It requires structured requirements, accessible context, clear quality controls, and human accountability across delivery. These themes are explored further in recent industry analysis State of AI-Native Software Engineering: 2026 Industry Report.
The strongest AI-assisted engineers will be those who recognize which work is ready for delegation, which work still requires close direction, and what must become clearer before autonomy can increase.
An AI agent earns autonomy when the task has been scoped tightly enough to make independent execution productive.
